source: World Bank Open Data + Claude editorial

GBR.text:
**Healthcare Spending vs. Outcomes**

The United Kingdom spends 18.6% of its budget on healthcare and achieves a life expectancy of 81.2 years with 4 infant deaths per 1,000 live births, while the US spends 25.9% of its budget and achieves 78.4 years life expectancy with 5.5 infant deaths per 1,000 live births. The UK provides universal coverage to 67 million people; the US leaves 27 million uninsured while spending $1.39 for every $1.00 the UK spends per capita on healthcare.

DEU.text: Germany spends 15.6% of its budget on healthcare and achieves a life expectancy of 80.5 years with 3.1 infant deaths per 1,000 births, while the US spends 25.9% of its budget and achieves 78.4 years life expectancy with 5.5 infant deaths per 1,000 births.

AUS.text: Australia achieves a life expectancy of 83.1 years and infant mortality of 3.1 per 1,000 births while spending 19.0% of its budget on healthcare, compared to the US spending 25.9% of its budget to achieve 78.4 years life expectancy and 5.5 per 1,000 infant mortality.

JPN.text: Japan achieves a life expectancy of 84.0 years and an infant mortality rate of 1.8 per 1,000 live births while spending 20.1% of its budget on healthcare, compared to the US spending 25.9% of its budget to achieve 78.4 years life expectancy and 5.5 per 1,000 infant mortality.

KOR.text: South Korea spends 14.8% of its budget on healthcare and achieves a life expectancy of 83.4 years with 2.3 infant deaths per 1,000 births, while the US spends 25.9% of its budget and achieves 78.4 years life expectancy with 5.5 infant deaths per 1,000 births.

FRA.text: **France spends 10.4 percentage points less of its budget on healthcare while achieving a life expectancy 4.5 years higher and infant mortality 38% lower than the US.** The US dedicates 25.9% of its budget to healthcare with 27 million uninsured, compared to France's 15.5% with universal coverage.

GBR.text: The United Kingdom allocates 11.5% of its budget to education compared to the US at 3.8%, yet both countries achieve nearly identical tertiary enrollment rates of approximately 80%.

DEU.text:
# Education Spending Callout

Germany allocates 11.3% of its budget to education compared to the US at 3.8%, yet both countries spend nearly identical shares of GDP on education (Germany 5.24%, US 5.42%) and achieve comparable tertiary enrollment rates (Germany 78.6%, US 79.4%).

AUS.text: Australia allocates 16.4% of its budget to education compared to the US at 3.8%, yet spends a slightly lower percentage of GDP on education (5.1% vs 5.4%). Australia achieves 104.6% tertiary enrollment compared to the US at 79.4%, indicating higher participation in higher education despite proportionally similar GDP investment.

JPN.text:
# Education Spending Callout

Japan allocates 9.0% of its budget to education compared to the US at 3.8%, yet spends 3.3% of GDP on education versus the US's 5.4%—achieving comparable tertiary enrollment rates (64.9% vs 79.4%) with significantly lower budget prioritization.

KOR.text:
# Education Spending Comparison

South Korea allocates 16.9% of its budget to education and achieves 106.7% tertiary enrollment, while the US dedicates 3.8% of its budget yet reaches 79.4% tertiary enrollment despite spending a similar share of GDP (5.8% vs. 5.4%).

FRA.text: France allocates 11.4% of its budget to education while the US allocates 3.8%, yet both countries spend nearly identical shares of GDP on education (France 5.3%, US 5.4%). The US achieves higher tertiary enrollment at 79.4% compared to France's 70.5%, despite directing a significantly smaller portion of its budget toward education.

GBR.text:
# Income Security Spending

The United Kingdom spends 13.1% of its budget on income security and achieves a Gini coefficient of 32.4, while the US spends 9.4% and has a Gini coefficient of 41.8—a 9.3-point gap in income inequality. The UK's bottom 20% earn 7.7% of national income compared to 5.1% in the US.

DEU.text:
# Income Security Spending

Germany spends 16.2% of its budget on income security and achieves a Gini coefficient of 32.4, while the US spends 9.4% and has a Gini coefficient of 41.8. The bottom 20% of earners in Germany capture 7.8% of national income compared to 5.1% in the US.

AUS.text:
# Income Security Spending

Australia spends 10.6% of its budget on income security compared to the US at 9.4%, yet achieves a Gini coefficient of 33.8 versus the US at 41.8, with the bottom 20% earning 7.3% of income in Australia versus 5.1% in the US.

JPN.text:
# Income Security Spending

Japan spends 16.0% of its budget on income security and achieves a Gini coefficient of 32.3, while the US spends 9.4% and has a Gini coefficient of 41.8. The bottom 20% of earners in Japan capture 6.9% of national income compared to 5.1% in the US.

KOR.text:
# Income Security Spending

South Korea spends 10.5% of its budget on income security and achieves a Gini index of 32.9, while the US spends 9.4% and has a Gini index of 41.8—meaning South Korea's bottom 20% earn 7.5% of national income compared to 5.1% in the US.

FRA.text:
# Income Security Spending

France allocates 16.4% of its budget to income security compared to the US at 9.4%, yet achieves a Gini coefficient of 31.8 versus the US at 41.8, with the bottom 20% earning 7.7% of income versus 5.1% in the US.

GBR.text: The United Kingdom allocates 10.8% of its budget to infrastructure while the US allocates 2.5%, yet the US achieves 38.15 broadband subscriptions per 100 people compared to the UK's 41.44 per 100 people.

DEU.text: Germany allocates 11.2% of its budget to infrastructure and achieves 45.38 broadband subscriptions per 100 people, compared to the US at 2.5% of budget spending and 38.15 subscriptions per 100 people.

AUS.text: Australia allocates 12.7% of its budget to infrastructure compared to the US at 2.5%, yet both countries achieve similar broadband penetration rates of approximately 37 per 100 people.

JPN.text: Japan allocates 11.7% of its budget to infrastructure compared to the US at 2.5%, yet both countries achieve nearly identical broadband penetration rates of approximately 38.6 and 38.1 per 100 people respectively.

KOR.text:
# Infrastructure Spending Callout

South Korea allocates 14.6% of its budget to infrastructure and achieves 46.6 broadband subscriptions per 100 people, compared to the US spending 2.5% of its budget while reaching 38.1 subscriptions per 100 people.

FRA.text: France allocates 11.6% of its budget to infrastructure and achieves 48.7 broadband subscriptions per 100 people, compared to the US spending 2.5% of its budget while achieving 38.1 subscriptions per 100 people.

GBR.text:
# Defense Spending

The United Kingdom spends 2.23% of GDP on defense while maintaining NATO's second-largest military, compared to the US spending 3.30% of GDP.

DEU.text:
# Defense Spending

Germany spends 1.49% of GDP on defense while achieving NATO's 2% capability threshold, compared to the US spending 3.30% of GDP. The US defense budget is approximately 2.2 times larger as a share of its economy than Germany's.

AUS.text:
# Defense Spending

Australia spends 5.8% of its budget on defense compared to the US at 12.8%, yet maintains a military that represents 1.86% of GDP versus the US at 3.30% of GDP.

JPN.text:
# Defense Spending

Japan spends 2.8% of its federal budget on defense while achieving a military capability that ranks among the world's top five, compared to the US spending 12.8% of its budget. Japan's defense spending represents 1.14% of GDP versus the US at 3.30% of GDP.

KOR.text: South Korea spends 2.6% of GDP on defense while maintaining the world's sixth-largest military and a technologically advanced force; the US spends 3.3% of GDP on defense.

FRA.text:
# Defense Spending

France spends 1.95% of GDP on defense while the US spends 3.30% of GDP—yet both are NATO members meeting security commitments with France allocating 3.2% of its budget versus the US at 12.8%.

GBR.text:
# Science & R&D Spending

The United Kingdom allocates 1.5% of its budget to science and R&D while spending 2.9% of GDP on research, compared to the US budget allocation of 1.1% with research spending at 3.6% of GDP.

---

*Note: This callout presents the data as provided, though it's worth noting these metrics measure different things (budget percentage vs. GDP percentage), which can make direct comparison complex. If you'd like a callout that focuses on a single comparable metric, I'd recommend clarifying which indicator should be the primary comparison point.*

DEU.text:
# Science & R&D Spending

Germany allocates 1.2% of its budget to science and R&D while spending 3.13% of GDP on research, compared to the US budget allocation of 1.1% despite higher R&D spending at 3.59% of GDP.

AUS.text:
# Science & R&D Spending

Australia allocates 2.3% of its budget to science and R&D compared to the US at 1.1%, yet the US invests 3.59% of GDP toward R&D while Australia invests 1.86% of GDP. The US achieves higher R&D intensity relative to its economy despite a lower budget allocation percentage.

JPN.text:
# Science & R&D Spending

Japan allocates 2.8% of its budget to science and R&D compared to the US at 1.1%, yet both countries invest roughly equivalent shares of GDP (Japan 3.41%, US 3.59%), indicating the US achieves similar R&D intensity through different budget prioritization.

KOR.text:
# Science & R&D Spending

South Korea invests 5.21% of GDP in R&D while spending 2.7% of its budget on science, compared to the US at 3.59% of GDP and 1.1% of budget allocation. South Korea's higher R&D intensity as a share of GDP has contributed to it ranking among the top three countries globally in patent filings per capita and semiconductor manufacturing capacity.

FRA.text: France allocates 1.8% of its budget to science and R&D while spending 2.23% of GDP on research, compared to the US allocation of 1.1% of budget despite investing 3.59% of GDP in R&D.
